title: Who Owns The Dreams Of An AI Machine?
text:
Innovation
AI
Who Owns The Dreams Of An AI Machine?
By
Charlie Fink
,
Contributor.
Forbes contributors publish independent expert analyses and insights.
A former tech executive covering AI and XR for Forbes.
Follow Author
Nov 13, 2025, 11:36am EST
Hollywood’s decline began long before artificial intelligence. Cable lost subscribers. Streaming saturated the market. Younger audiences shifted to games and social platforms. The cost of scripted production rose as viewership fell. AI entered an already contracting ecosystem. It did not cause the crisis, but it exacerbates it.
When production company Secret Level can make an elaborate Coke commercial for 10% of what it would have cost just three years ago, one sees workers’ anxiety is not unfounded. On the other hand, three years ago no one, not even Coke, had the budget for a production of this scale. No technicians lost work, since the work would not have otherwise existed without this budget-busting new technology. If twenty-five can make a commercial like this, eventually five can. And eventually, maybe not too long from now, the machine will make it without our help at all.
Angela Dunning, a partner at Cleary Gottlieb who argued some of the first AI copyright cases, told
The AI/XR Podcast
in May that U.S. law is not prepared for this kind of authorship. “Copyright protects human inventorship,” she said. “If a person is directing the creative process, deciding composition, pacing, lighting, those decisions still count as authorship.” She predicted that courts would eventually draw a distinction between
using
a model and
delegating
creativity to one. The key test is control.
Angela Duning, Esq., one of Hollywood's leading intellectual property expects. She has already argued a number of AI cases.
Cleary, Gottlieb
Dunning’s view foreshadowed the rulings that came later. In
Authors Guild v. Anthropic
, Judge William Alsup decided that training a model on text publicly available online was legal as long as the data was not retained. The liability appeared only when Anthropic kept a library of those texts. In London, the High Court dismissed Getty Images’ complaint against Stability AI because the company’s model did not reproduce the photographs it learned from. Dunning had argued that training a model to understand how images and words relate qualifies as fair use, not copying. Those courts agreed.
MORE FOR YOU
I prompted Sora 2 to create a scene from a sci-fi western, featuring myself as an old Sheriff (Sora will insert you if you ask) and a gang of  anthropomorphic animal  gunfighters. I was clearly directing with
my prompt
, choosing pacing, framing, and character. Contrasting this, when I asked Sora to take me back to the split-level suburb of my childhood, it placed me into some kind of “Back to the Future” scenario, where I meet my future parents. “Mom, it's me,” said my avatar. “I’m your son, Charlie. I know it sounds insane, but I’m from the future.” When the short starts, I'm even standing next to a glowing portal, implying the familiar “Back to the Future” and “Terminator” time travel trope. One video was clearly authored. The other was clearly made by AI. The difference was control of the specific versus the embrace of the random.
I feel ownership over the delightfully derivative and imaginative short film only because I’m in it, but my avatar is clearly being puppeteered by a random, supernatural force that I don’t fully understand (note: no one does). I could take over the creative process, using this as inspiration, and make my “Back to the Future” knockoff like I made my sci-fi western. That would change things. I can own that.
Dunning compared this spectrum to how young filmmakers mix generative tools with traditional craft. “They’re creating proofs of concept,” she said, “but they still make the creative calls.” The law, she added, must evolve to see authorship as layered rather than binary.
The U.S. Copyright Office’s recent decisions follow that thinking. It has reaffirmed that works made entirely by AI cannot be registered, but hybrid works can be, provided human input shapes the result. That question is now central to the lawsuit brought by Jason M. Allen, whose AI-assisted artwork
Théâtre d’Opéra Spatial
won a Colorado State Fair competition in 2022. The agency revoked his registration, arguing his contribution was minimal. Allen sued. Cases like his are expensive, often requiring corporate or advocacy backing. They are less about one artist’s rights than about setting policy for an entire creative economy.
Every AI-generated scene blends human decisions with machine patterning. Tracking those contributions will determine who gets credit and who gets paid. Provenance is no longer a by-product of storytelling; it is part of the story itself. As Dunning said, “The law follows the human hand. Wherever that hand still touches the work, that’s where authorship begins.”
The business side of that economy is changing faster than the law. Cost compression is reorganizing production. Screen work is fragmenting into five categories: social-media and native microdramas, reality formats built around real people, large-scale theatrical releases, smaller hybrid productions that combine AI with live actors, and traditional star-driven blockbusters. Which form dominates will shift. AI production is still niche today. It has not reached an inflection point, where it is impossible to tell what is real, but I have no doubt it will, probably sooner than later.
